title: Does CLIP’s generalization performance mainly stem from high train-test similarity?

reviews.summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.
reviews.strengths: The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.
reviews.weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.
reviews.questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Strengths:
The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.

Weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.

1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper investigates the reason behind the exceptional generalization performance of foundation models like CLIP, particularly its high zero-shot accuracy on out-of-distribution (OOD) datasets. The authors hypothesize that CLIP's performance may be attributed to the presence of similar images in its large pre-training dataset, LAION, rather than the models' ability to generalize.

The authors define a notion of perceptual similarity between images using CLIP's ViT-B/16 embeddings and find that common OOD datasets, such as ImageNet Sketch, have more similar images with LAION than with ImageNet. To systematically test this hypothesis, they conduct two experiments. First, they iteratively remove images similar to the OOD dataset from LAION and measure the performance of models trained on the pruned dataset. As expected, they observe a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset have more importance.

The second experiment compares models trained on small datasets, such as ImageNet, with those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which represents the minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. By removing data points from LAION to match the generalization gap of ImageNet with the OOD dataset, they find that CLIP's performance drops but remains high compared to models trained solely on ImageNet. This leads them to conclude that CLIP's high performance is mostly due to more training data rather than test-train similarity.

One of the strengths of this paper is its clear and well-defined experiments, which provide valuable insights into CLIP's generalization performance. The authors are careful in evaluating both the strengths and weaknesses of their work. However, as noted in Alternative Review 1, the paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion on the choice of ImageNet and its distribution shifts as the main object of study. Additionally, providing more clarity on the notion of highly similar images and exploring other factors that contribute to CLIP's generalization performance, such as data diversity and density, would further strengthen the paper.

A limitation of the study is that the experiments are primarily conducted on variations of the ImageNet dataset. As pointed out in the main review, it would be beneficial to include experiments with other OOD datasets, such as iWILDCam or FMoW, to ensure that the analysis is not limited to one type of dataset. This is a crucial concern, as the results may not generalize to other types of OOD datasets.

The authors' method of pruning LAION-400M to remove highly similar images is a significant contribution, as noted in Alternative Review 3. However, it would be interesting to see how CLIP's performance compares to other models on the same benchmarks and how the proposed method relates to other methods for deduplication and pruning.

In terms of the definition of highly similar images, the authors rely on CLIP's ViT-B/16 embeddings. Alternative Review 2 raises a valid question about whether larger CLIP models would have a better embedding to filter out data points. This is an important consideration, as the choice of model and embedding can significantly impact the results.

Overall, the paper provides a thorough analysis of CLIP's generalization performance and its relation to train-test similarity. While there are some limitations, the authors' conclusions are well-supported by their experiments. With some additional exploration of other OOD datasets and factors contributing to CLIP's performance, this paper could be even more comprehensive.

Given the significance of the findings and the thorough analysis presented, I recommend a rating of 6, marginally above the acceptance threshold. The paper provides valuable insights into CLIP's generalization performance, and with some revisions to address the concerns mentioned above, it has the potential to be an excellent addition to the field.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.
reviews.questions: What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

This paper investigates whether CLIP's exceptional generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, specifically the similarity between the training and test distributions. The authors tackle this question by filtering out similar samples from the LAION-400M dataset based on CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. They create a subset of LAION-400M that replicates the train-test similarity of ImageNet-1K and find that CLIP's overall performance remains high even when trained on this curated subset. This suggests that factors other than train-test similarity contribute to CLIP's outstanding out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization.

The paper's perspective on studying the OOD generalization behavior of CLIP from the viewpoint of train-test distribution similarity is interesting and well-motivated, as noted by Alternative Review 1. The authors draw intriguing conclusions, such as a 100M subset of LAION-400M being sufficient to train a good-performing model on OOD benchmarks. The experiments are well-designed and yield compelling results, showing that CLIP maintains good performance on OOD benchmarks even without nearest neighbor samples.

However, as pointed out in the main review, the paper primarily focuses on common classification OOD benchmarks, neglecting other CLIP application domains, such as retrieval. Alternative Review 2 suggests using MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering and Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Additionally, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB, would provide a more comprehensive understanding of CLIP's generalization capabilities. The authors could also benefit from a more detailed discussion on the implications of their findings and the potential limitations of their study, as noted by Alternative Review 2.

A crucial aspect that requires further clarification is the construction of the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1. The main review notes that this detail is missing, and Alternative Review 3 suggests providing more information on the pruning process and its effect on CLIP's performance. Furthermore, comparing the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION dataset and ImageNet-Train would help address concerns about LAION's potential unfair advantage on OOD benchmarks.

The authors' formal definition of highly similar images and proposed method to systematically test the hypothesis that CLIP's performance stems from highly similar images are notable strengths, as highlighted by Alternative Reviews 2 and 3. However, the writing could be improved for clarity and organization, as suggested by Alternative Review 4.

Regarding the potential application of the finding in this paper, it would be interesting to explore how this work enlightens future research or engineering endeavors. The authors could discuss the broader impact of their findings, such as implications for the development of future models or the importance of data diversity and density.

In conclusion, while the paper provides a thorough analysis of CLIP's generalization performance and its relation to train-test similarity, it requires some revisions to address the concerns mentioned above. Specifically, the authors should provide more experimental details, explore diverse benchmarks, and discuss the implications of their findings.

reviews.summary: The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.
reviews.strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"
reviews.questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Summary

The paper investigates whether the superior out-of-distribution (OOD) performance of foundation models like CLIP is primarily due to the training dataset containing images very similar to the OOD test sets. The authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset used for training OpenCLIP. They find that pruning samples similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors conclude that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet-pretrained model. This suggests that although LAION contains images similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for CLIP's better OOD generalization.

## Strengths

The paper presents several insightful experimental results, which are helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are computationally intensive. The authors' key finding—that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance—is particularly noteworthy. The release of the pruned datasets can further aid investigation into why CLIP models have better OOD performance. As noted by Alternative Review 1, the paper is technically sound, and the methods used are appropriate. The results are important and likely to be used by researchers.

## Weaknesses

A major concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair OOD testing. For OOD evaluation to be fair, it is crucial that all images from the test domain are removed from the train set, not just those similar to every test set image. As highlighted in the main review, if there are multiple sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset and only images close to these sketches are removed, other airplane sketches farther away from the closest ImageNet test set image may remain. This could unfairly influence the OOD evaluation. Furthermore, the domain "sketch" may not be entirely unseen by CLIP if sketches of other objects, like "space shuttle," are present in the training data. 

## Suggestions for Improvement

To address the weaknesses, the authors should consider additional experiments that emulate a true OOD setting. For instance, they could remove all "sketch" style images from the LAION-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) dataset to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a truly OOD evaluation. This would help validate their findings and provide a more comprehensive understanding of CLIP's generalization capabilities.

## Minor Feedback

The clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. A shorter summary of the key contributions would be beneficial. Additionally, renaming "generalization gap" to something more specific to the context would enhance clarity. The authors should also consider providing more details on the pruning process and its effect on CLIP's performance, as suggested by Alternative Review 2.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, while the paper presents valuable insights into CLIP's OOD performance and its relation to train-test similarity, it falls short in fully addressing the challenges of fair OOD evaluation. With additional experiments and refinements to address these concerns, the paper could make a more compelling contribution to the field. Despite these limitations, the paper's strengths and contributions warrant a rating of 6, marginally above the acceptance threshold.

## Rating

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.
reviews.strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?
reviews.questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Summary:
This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

reviews.text:
This paper investigates the hypothesis that CLIP's accuracy on test sets mainly stems from highly similar images in its train set. The authors take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to out-of-distribution (OOD) benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. 

The paper presents a thorough analysis of CLIP's generalization performance and its relation to train-test similarity. A key strength of the paper is its comprehensive set of experiments, including the evaluation of CLIP's performance on various OOD benchmarks, such as ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R. For instance, Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not. Additionally, Figure 3 quantitatively shows that the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set, except for the ImageNet-Val set itself.

However, there are some areas that require further exploration. The authors could provide more clarity on the notion of highly similar images and how it is defined, as suggested by Alternative Review 1. This would help to better understand the implications of the results and the potential limitations of the study. Furthermore, it would be interesting to see how CLIP's performance compares to other models on the same benchmarks, as mentioned in Alternative Review 2.

The authors observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. They also show that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in a substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples, as seen in Figure 4. This suggests that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

To further strengthen the paper, the authors could discuss the choice of ImageNet and its distribution shifts as the main object of study, as suggested by Alternative Review 1. Additionally, they could explore other factors that contribute to CLIP's generalization performance, such as data diversity and density. The authors could also provide more insights into the specific properties of the training data that drive CLIP's performance, as mentioned in Alternative Review 2.

One of the main weaknesses of the paper is that the results in Figure 4 are not conclusive. The authors could perform additional experiments to address two key questions: (i) Does a model only lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? and (ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? These experiments would help to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the impact of pruning on CLIP's performance.

The paper could also benefit from a broader discussion on "What is zero-shot?" and "What is out-of-distribution robustness?" as mentioned in the main review. After showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6, which aims to "Correct for highly similar images," is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set?

In terms of potential implications, the authors could discuss how their findings relate to other methods for deduplication and pruning, as mentioned in Alternative Review 3. Additionally, they could explore the potential applications of their proposed method for pruning LAION-400M.

Overall, the paper presents a novel analysis of CLIP's generalization performance and its relation to train-test similarity. While there are some areas that require further exploration, the paper is technically sound, and the methods used are appropriate. However, based on the current evidence, I would rate this paper a 5, marginally below the acceptance threshold. The authors have made a good effort in evaluating both the strengths and weaknesses of their work, but some additional experiments and discussions are needed to strengthen the paper and provide a more comprehensive understanding of the implications of their findings. 

There are a few typos and grammatical errors, such as on page 7: "Let us for consider" which should be "Let us consider". 

In conclusion, while the paper has some strengths, it is marginally below the acceptance threshold due to the need for additional experiments and discussions to fully support the claims made.

title: Simple Hierarchical Planning with Diffusion

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.
reviews.strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.
reviews.weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.
reviews.questions: See weakness above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. This approach combines the advantages of hierarchical and diffusion-based planning, achieving superior performance and efficiency on offline reinforcement learning benchmarks.

One of the key strengths of HD is its ability to discretize planning into sub-goals with diffusion models, which is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic, allowing for improved receptibility, OOD generalization, and reduced computation overhead compared to the original Diffuser model. For instance, the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than Diffuser due to its increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of Diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

However, the formulation has limited novelty, as it builds upon the existing Diffuser model by introducing two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning the sequence of sub-goals between the initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments. This raises questions about the necessity of using both state and action diffusion, as opposed to state-only diffusion methods like Decision-diffuser. It would be beneficial to perform relevant ablations to justify why states and actions are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$. Additionally, it is essential to clarify whether the authors are using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks, similar to Diffuser.

The Hierarchical Diffuser demonstrates superior performance and efficiency on various benchmarks, including Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym, and FrankKitchen. The results show that HD outperforms previous methods, including relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. However, the choice of fixed sub-goal intervals simplifies the model's architecture but might fall short in handling complex real-world scenarios. It would be interesting to explore how the choice of K affects the performance of Hierarchical Diffuser.

Another potential limitation of HD is that solving for sub-goals independently without knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. The authors should provide more details on how they ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for other segments. Furthermore, it is essential to clarify how many trajectories are sampled for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence.

In terms of computational efficiency, HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments. However, the model's performance is restricted by the coverage or quality of datasets, and it may struggle to produce optimal plans in unfamiliar trajectories.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser, which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough to warrant a higher rating. The paper provides a thorough evaluation, and the submission is technically sound, clearly written, and well-organized. Nevertheless, the limitations mentioned above prevent me from recommending a higher rating.

Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper proposes an hierarchical variant of Diffuser. The algorithm involves using a high-level planner that plans trajectories in a sparser manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produces by the high-level planner. Experiment result demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks. The authers also study the impact of kernel size in Diffuser and the impact of the jumpy steps. An interesting observation is that Diffuser with a small receptive field can not cover the data distribution well.
reviews.strengths:
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The method is straightforward and demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially the AntMaze.
3. The study of relationship between receptive field of the diffusion model and the data coverage ratio is interesting and offers valuable insight for futher research on diffusion models for planning in more complex domains.
reviews.weaknesses:
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser[1] is missing. Also the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecutre, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.
reviews.questions:
1. According to Fig 3., Diffuser with a small kernel size covers one mode of the data distribution during actual execution. Is it the case that the standard Diffuser is able to \textbf{cover} the full data distribution but just fails to achieve full data coverage during actual execution? If so, what might be the cause the such gap?
2. Still concerning Fig 3., although HD seems to cover most of the data distribution, HD fails to cover two short segments/branches. Why does such pheomena occur?
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

The paper proposes an hierarchical variant of Diffuser. The algorithm involves using a high-level planner that plans trajectories in a sparser manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produces by the high-level planner. Experiment result demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks. 1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The method is straightforward and demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially the AntMaze.
3. The study of relationship between receptive field of the diffusion model and the data coverage ratio is interesting and offers valuable insight for futher research on diffusion models for planning in more complex domains.

Weaknesses:
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser[1] is missing. Also the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecutre, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.

Questions:
1. According to Fig 3., Diffuser with a small kernel size covers one mode of the data distribution during actual execution. Is it the case that the standard Diffuser is able to \textbf{cover} the full data distribution but just fails to achieve full data coverage during actual execution? If so, what might be the cause the such gap?
2. Still concerning Fig 3., although HD seems to cover most of the data distribution, HD fails to cover two short segments/branches. Why does such pheomena occur?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Hybrid Review**

**Summary:**
The paper proposes a hierarchical variant of Diffuser, which combines the strengths of hierarchical and diffusion-based planning. The algorithm involves a high-level planner that generates trajectories in a sparse manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produced by the high-level planner. The experimental results demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks.

**Strengths:**
The paper is well-written and easy to follow, presenting a straightforward and effective method that demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially in AntMaze. The study on the relationship between the receptive field of the diffusion model and the data coverage ratio is interesting and offers valuable insights for further research on diffusion models for planning in more complex domains. As noted by Alternative Review 2, the submission is technically sound, with well-supported claims and appropriate methods, providing a complete piece of work with a thorough evaluation.

**Weaknesses:**
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser is missing. Additionally, the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture, as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecture, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged, as pointed out by the main review. Furthermore, Alternative Review 4 highlights the paper's dependency on the quality of the dataset and the choice of fixed sub-goal intervals as limitations. The authors could explore more complex real-world scenarios where fixed sub-goal intervals may not be effective.

**Questions:**
The main review raises an important question regarding Fig 3: According to Fig 3, Diffuser with a small kernel size covers one mode of the data distribution during actual execution. Is it the case that the standard Diffuser is able to cover the full data distribution but just fails to achieve full data coverage during actual execution? If so, what might be the cause of such a gap? Alternative Review 4 also poses relevant questions: How does the Hierarchical Diffuser perform with different types of datasets, especially those with varying quality or coverage? Can the Hierarchical Diffuser be adapted to handle more complex real-world scenarios where fixed sub-goal intervals may not be effective?

**Methodology and Results:**
The paper presents a novel combination of hierarchical reinforcement learning and diffusion-based planning, which is a valuable contribution to the field, as noted by Alternative Reviews 2 and 3. The results demonstrate a significant improvement over previous state-of-the-art methods, and the submission is clearly written and well-organized, providing enough information for an expert reader to reproduce the results.

**Conclusion and Rating:**
Overall, the paper proposes a simple and effective planning method that combines hierarchical and diffusion-based planning, achieving superior performance and efficiency on offline reinforcement learning benchmarks. While there are some limitations, the paper provides a thorough analysis and theoretical justification for the proposed method. Based on the strengths and weaknesses discussed, I maintain a rating of 6, marginally above the acceptance threshold. The authors are encouraged to address the minor weaknesses and questions raised to further improve the paper. 

Rating: 6

reviews.summary: The authors of this paper present Hierarchical Diffuser, a simple yet effective hierarchical planning approach using diffusion models to improve the performance and efficiency of diffusion-based planning for long-horizon tasks. By utilizing a two-pronged structure, the Hierarchical Diffuser combines a high-level planner, the Sparse Diffuser, to generate sub-goals, and a low-level planner to refine the plan. This approach significantly outperforms non-hierarchical planning methods, while also enhancing computational efficiency in training and planning speed. Furthermore, the method displays improved generalization capabilities on out-of-distribution tasks compared to non-hierarchical Diffusers.
reviews.strengths:
- Originality: This work creatively combines the strengths of hierarchical planning and diffusion models, achieving significant improvements in performance and computational efficiency compared to existing methods.
- Quality: The contribution provides a thorough theoretical analysis of the method, backed by well-designed experiments and in-depth analyses. The authors emphasize the rationale behind the design decisions and the resultant benefits of their approach.
- Clarity: The paper is well-organized, clearly written, and explains the method along with its advantages and limitations in a coherent and accessible way.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Considering the existence of the HDMI algorithm, the contribution of this paper are limited. However, compared to HDMI, this paper has conducted extensive experiments on generalization and provided corresponding theoretical support. One suggestion I have is that the authors can emphasize the advantages of Hierarchical Diffuser from the perspective of generalization, and highlight the algorithm's generalization performance in the experimental design.
- In the hierarchical framework, the quality of the generated high-level goals is crucial to the effectiveness of the algorithm. The authors emphasize that the Hierarchical Diffuser is simpler compared to existing hierarchical methods but have only designed one goal selection method. In addition to the fixed time interval heuristic method used in this paper, HDMI also mentions two heuristic methods based on spatial intervals and reward scale, which are equally simple and have low implementation costs. If the authors could take these heuristic methods into consideration, I believe it would have a positive impact on the completeness of the paper's content. Furthermore, the demonstration of the quality of the generated goals is essential, and there is a gap in this aspect in the current paper.
reviews.questions:
- I have noticed that both Diffuser and Decision Diffuser employ “inpainting” techniques for trajectory generation in goal-conditioned tasks and the lower-level planner of HDMI. However, this paper utilizes return-guided sampling. What were the considerations behind the authors' choice between the two methods?
- This paper proposes to get the sub-goals first with the planning-based subgoal extractor and then train HDMI with the sub-goals as supervision. Although this two-step process makes sense, the overall training process becomes ad hoc and less general. Is there any connection between the upper and lower layers of Hierarchical Diffuser's planner? Can it be trained end-to-end?

The authors of this paper present Hierarchical Diffuser, a simple yet effective hierarchical planning approach using diffusion models to improve the performance and efficiency of diffusion-based planning for long-horizon tasks. By utilizing a two-pronged structure, the Hierarchical Diffuser combines a high-level planner, the Sparse Diffuser, to generate sub-goals, and a low-level planner to refine the plan. This approach significantly outperforms non-hierarchical planning methods, while also enhancing computational efficiency in training and planning speed. Furthermore, the method displays improved generalization capabilities on out-of-distribution tasks compared to non-hierarchical Diffusers.

Strengths:
- Originality: This work creatively combines the strengths of hierarchical planning and diffusion models, achieving significant improvements in performance and computational efficiency compared to existing methods.
- Quality: The contribution provides a thorough theoretical analysis of the method, backed by well-designed experiments and in-depth analyses. The authors emphasize the rationale behind the design decisions and the resultant benefits of their approach.
- Clarity: The paper is well-organized, clearly written, and explains the method along with its advantages and limitations in a coherent and accessible way.

Weaknesses:
- Considering the existence of the HDMI algorithm, the contribution of this paper are limited. However, compared to HDMI, this paper has conducted extensive experiments on generalization and provided corresponding theoretical support. One suggestion I have is that the authors can emphasize the advantages of Hierarchical Diffuser from the perspective of generalization, and highlight the algorithm's generalization performance in the experimental design.
- In the hierarchical framework, the quality of the generated high-level goals is crucial to the effectiveness of the algorithm. The authors emphasize that the Hierarchical Diffuser is simpler compared to existing hierarchical methods but have only designed one goal selection method. In addition to the fixed time interval heuristic method used in this paper, HDMI also mentions two heuristic methods based on spatial intervals and reward scale, which are equally simple and have low implementation costs. If the authors could take these heuristic methods into consideration, I believe it would have a positive impact on the completeness of the paper's content. Furthermore, the demonstration of the quality of the generated goals is essential, and there is a gap in this aspect in the current paper.

Questions:
- I have noticed that both Diffuser and Decision Diffuser employ “inpainting” techniques for trajectory generation in goal-conditioned tasks and the lower-level planner of HDMI. However, this paper utilizes return-guided sampling. What were the considerations behind the authors' choice between the two methods?
- This paper proposes to get the sub-goals first with the planning-based subgoal extractor and then train HDMI with the sub-goals as supervision. Although this two-step process makes sense, the overall training process becomes ad hoc and less general. Is there any connection between the upper and lower layers of Hierarchical Diffuser's planner? Can it be trained end-to-end?

reviews.text:
The authors of this paper present Hierarchical Diffuser, a simple yet effective hierarchical planning approach using diffusion models to improve the performance and efficiency of diffusion-based planning for long-horizon tasks. By utilizing a two-pronged structure, the Hierarchical Diffuser combines a high-level planner, the Sparse Diffuser, to generate sub-goals, and a low-level planner to refine the plan. This approach significantly outperforms non-hierarchical planning methods, while also enhancing computational efficiency in training and planning speed. Furthermore, the method displays improved generalization capabilities on out-of-distribution tasks compared to non-hierarchical Diffusers.

The paper is well-organized, clearly written, and explains the method along with its advantages and limitations in a coherent and accessible way. The contribution provides a thorough theoretical analysis of the method, backed by well-designed experiments and in-depth analyses. The authors emphasize the rationale behind the design decisions and the resultant benefits of their approach. As noted in Alternative Review 2 and Alternative Review 3, the combination of hierarchical reinforcement learning and diffusion-based planning is novel and presents a valuable contribution to the field.

One of the strengths of this work is its originality in creatively combining the strengths of hierarchical planning and diffusion models. This results in significant improvements in performance and computational efficiency compared to existing methods. The paper is technically sound, with well-supported claims and appropriate methods, providing a complete piece of work with a thorough evaluation. The results are important, and others are likely to use the ideas or build on them.

However, as pointed out in the main review, considering the existence of the HDMI algorithm, the contribution of this paper is somewhat limited. Nevertheless, this paper has conducted extensive experiments on generalization and provided corresponding theoretical support. To further strengthen the paper, the authors could emphasize the advantages of Hierarchical Diffuser from the perspective of generalization and highlight the algorithm's generalization performance in the experimental design. Additionally, as suggested in Alternative Review 4, the paper could benefit from a discussion on how the Hierarchical Diffuser performs with different types of datasets, especially those with varying quality or coverage.

The quality of the generated high-level goals is crucial to the effectiveness of the algorithm. While the authors emphasize that the Hierarchical Diffuser is simpler compared to existing hierarchical methods, they have only designed one goal selection method. As mentioned in the main review, considering alternative heuristic methods such as those based on spatial intervals and reward scale, as mentioned in HDMI, could positively impact the completeness of the paper's content. Furthermore, demonstrating the quality of the generated goals is essential, and there is a gap in this aspect in the current paper.

The authors could also address a few questions to further clarify their work. For instance, the choice between "inpainting" techniques used in Diffuser and Decision Diffuser, and the return-guided sampling used in this paper. As noted in Alternative Review 3, it would be beneficial to understand how the choice of K affects the performance of Hierarchical Diffuser. Additionally, exploring how the Hierarchical Diffuser handles cases where the sub-goals are not achievable, as suggested in Alternative Review 3, would provide further insight into its robustness.

In conclusion, while there are some limitations to the Hierarchical Diffuser, the paper presents a significant contribution to the field by combining hierarchical planning and diffusion models. The thorough analysis, well-designed experiments, and clear writing make a strong case for the method's effectiveness. With a rating of 6, marginally above the acceptance threshold, the paper is worthy of acceptance, pending minor revisions to address the points discussed above.

Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

The Hierarchical Diffuser incorporates a two-tiered approach: a high-level "jumpy" planning strategy that can quickly survey a broader scope of possibilities with reduced computational demands, and a low-level planner that refines these broader plans into specific, actionable steps. This hierarchy allows the method to operate more efficiently and effectively, with the higher level providing guidance that simplifies the task for the lower level. Empirical evaluations show that this method outperforms both traditional diffusion-based planners and other hierarchical approaches in speed and performance on standard offline reinforcement learning benchmarks.

This hierarchy allows the method to operate more efficiently and effectively, with the higher level providing guidance that simplifies the task for the lower level. Empirical evaluations show that this method outperforms both traditional diffusion-based planners and other hierarchical approaches in speed and performance on standard offline reinforcement learning benchmarks.

Strengths:

- In replanning, a hierarchical structure is more efficient since it only needs to use low-level to

Weaknesses:

reviews.text:
This paper proposes a modified version of the diffuser called Hierarchical Diffuser, which incorporates a two-tiered approach: a high-level "jumpy" planning strategy and a low-level planner. The hierarchical structure makes sense in long-horizon planning, as it allows the method to operate more efficiently and effectively. The high-level planner provides guidance that simplifies the task for the lower-level planner. 

The authors have provided empirical evaluations showing that this method outperforms both traditional diffusion-based planners and other hierarchical approaches in speed and performance on standard offline reinforcement learning benchmarks. However, as noted by Alternative Review 2, the model's performance is restricted by the coverage or quality of datasets, and it may struggle to produce optimal plans in unfamiliar trajectories.

One of the strengths of the paper is its thorough evaluation, which includes a complete analysis of the method's performance on various benchmarks. The submission is technically sound, with well-supported claims and appropriate methods, as noted by Alternative Review 2. However, the paper could benefit from more discussion on the choice of K and its effect on the performance of Hierarchical Diffuser, as questioned by Alternative Reviews 2 and 3.

The main review highlights that current SOTA diffusers seem to have better performance, citing [1] with a 167 score on large maze2d. The authors might need to provide more results to show that they are better. Alternative Review 4 notes that the paper has some limitations, including its dependency on the quality of the dataset and the choice of fixed sub-goal intervals. 

The paper claims to have evaluated generalization, but the OOD task they test on is too simple. As Alternative Review 1 implies, more complex OOD tasks, such as adding coins or obstacles, should be tested to fully demonstrate the method's capabilities. For instance, [1] and [2] have tested more challenging OOD tasks, which should be considered for future evaluations.

The improvement in Mujoco is not enough to convince the reviewer, and the Unet itself has a hierarchical structure. If an environment needs a hierarchical structure, a simple way is to increase the depth of the Unet. The authors might need to provide more results to show that their method is better. 

The Hierarchical Diffuser seems to be a valuable contribution to the field, combining the advantages of hierarchical and diffusion-based planning. However, the reviewer still has concerns about the significance of the improvement, particularly with regards to the SD-DA, which seems to only improve the performance of walker2d. 

Given these considerations, the reviewer maintains a rating of 5, marginally below the acceptance threshold. While the paper presents a novel combination of hierarchical and diffusion-based planning and demonstrates superior performance and efficiency on various benchmarks, it requires more thorough evaluations and comparisons to existing methods to be considered for acceptance. 

Rating: 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: NECO: NEural Collapse Based Out-of-distribution detection

reviews.summary: In this paper, a novel post-hoc OOD detection method, NECO, is presented. NECO utilizes the geometric properties of neural collapse (NC) and principal component space to identify OOD data. This paper hypothesizes that NC not only affects the convergence of the loss during training but also impacts OOD detection.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper is clearly motivated and well-written.
2. This paper is innovative in the novel observation of ID/OOD orthogonality(NC5).
3. This paper provides thorough theoretical analysis.
4. Experimental results on the ImageNet and CIFAR benchmarks demonstrate that NECO is state-of-the-art. And NECO can be generalized to various backbones.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The NECO score is similar to the ViM score. The main difference is that the NECO score utilizes principal space features, while the ViM score utilizes null space features for OOD detection.
2. The manuscript lacks critical baseline comparisons, such as [1]. It is recommended that the authors conduct a comparison with it to provide a more comprehensive evaluation.

[1] Song, Yue, Nicu Sebe, and Wei Wang. "Rankfeat: Rank-1 feature removal for out-of-distribution detection." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 35 (2022): 17885-17898.
reviews.questions: Why not put NuSA in the baseline? Can NECO outperform NuSA?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
